speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Lyon, would you continue with the story about you and your friend Karl Lyon in Kansas City, Missouri? What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, I fell deeply in love [LAUGHS] that evening. And I was afraid nothing will come of it. And but I moved out to Los Angeles when my aunt decided to stay there. And, of course, since I resided with her in Kansas City, and she brought me out here, it was a natural transition. And I looked forward to living in Los Angeles. And, of course, I was closer to San Francisco, where someone I loved was going to law school, in San Francisco. And so I was hoping that perhaps this will nurture the relationship. That summer, we corresponded. And I didn't speak English, but I was learning. Consequently, every letter-- every word in each letter was looked up in a dictionary. However, the sentences and the sentence structure is quite chaotic. And now we can laugh about it when we look at those letters 47-plus years later. Indeed I saw Karl, and we got engaged in January of 1949. And on August 17th, when he had a school break after his second year of law school, we married in Los Angeles. And immediately, we-- well, we flew up to the Santa Cruz mountains, where we had our honeymoon. And I moved to San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us a little about Karl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karl is-- was born in Germany in Ba-- in Buhl, in the state of Baden, near, near Baden-Baden, in southern Germany. In 1937, he, he fled Nazi Germany when it no longer was safe for him there. His parents thought that he has no future there, and this is the right move. And at-- so at age 15, he went to Kansas City, Missouri, where his uncle lived, his father's brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Karl left behind his father, Alfred Lyon, and his mother, Joan Lyon. Actually, his real mother passed aw-- passed away when he was six years old. Her name was Hermina Lyon. And then my father-in-law married Joan Lyon, and they-- and they had a son. So Karl has a half brother, Martin Lyon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we get back just a little moment? Since you're speaking of the past and in Europe, perhaps you'd like to describe the events that happened with your family who remained in Czechoslovakia after the war. Where are they now, your immediate family, your siblings? I-- don't you want me to finish Karl for a few more-- just a few more thoughts? If you-- oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I just want to say in a few short words that at the age of 17, Karl volunteered to fight in World War II. But he was turned down because he came from an enemy alien country. But true Army style, they drafted him. So he served in the US Armed Forces in the field of artillery. And he fought in the European theatre. In France, he landed in Marseilles. And in Germany, he came within 10 miles from his hometown, at which time they had to-- the American forces had to retreat, and they crossed the Rhine at some other point into Germany, and then to Austria. And when he came home, he, he went to Kansas City. But his father landed in Dachau-- was taken to Dachau on Kristallnacht-- Crystal Night. And he was released a few months later, when my mother-in-law could prove that they will leave the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left the country and went to Cuba on the advice of Senator Harry S. Truman, a friend of the family in Kansas City. And there they waited for their visa, which they were advised to do, for 13 months before entering the United States. And they joined Karl. My mother-in-law is 101 years old now. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My family in, in Europe-- well, this, this very complicated part of the world, which became the Soviet Union, was-- they were just stuck there. Finally, in 1962, after many, many tries of visiting them, I was permitted to visit them for the first time after a hiatus of 17 years. And but not in our hometown. They were not-- they wouldn't let anybody in. It was restricted. It was border area, heavily fortified. They didn't want the world to be exposed to that area. Consequently, I was permitted to go to Lviv-- formerly Lemberg, formerly belonged to Poland-- approximately 200 miles from where I lived. And there, we saw each other for the first time, after 17 years. I-- this is the first time I met my sister-in-laws, I thought, only to find that they were with me in Auschwitz. They both remembered me. And they were right there near my bunk beds. And so, I, I was so pleased. They each had three children. One was about to become bar mitzvahed in the middle of the night, at 1:00 in the morning. And so it was very exciting. I even went to a synagogue in, in what was called Leningrad at the time, on the way there, where I had to spend a day coming and going. It was very interesting-- very interesting experience for that period. I-- on the way back, I visited the Swedish family for the first time since I lived with them. And they gave me a dinner party, and the whole family was invited. And I submit a picture for your perusal. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My immediate family, after I left, was my brother Michael, his wife, three children, and, and his mother-in-law lived with them. And Shandor, his wife, Sharika, and their three children. And Annuska, her husband, Dudi Herskovits, and their three children. And my father, of course. I made up my mind that I will never rest until I bring my family out of there to this free country. And when I came back, that is the first thing I attended to. And it was a period when nobody had any luck. And I was very persistent. I tried, and tried again. And, and finally, I think they got tired of me. They permitted my father to come for a visit after trying him to come on a permanent basis. And when he was here, as soon as he stepped off the plane, and we met, he said, I will never return. However, our doctor was able to help out, and, and he developed a heart problem. He actually came here with it, which he didn't know. And so, we were able to use that as the reason for him not returning. For every year, he received a letter in Russian, when are you coming back, from the authorities. After Dad came, we brought my sister over and her husband and three children. And following that, my brother, his wife, and three children, and then my other brother and his family. And so it-- there were all sorts of complications. And they're all here in this wonderful land of freedom, really working very hard. And they put their pennies together. They didn't even buy themselves a ticket to a theater, or a concert. They wanted everybody who worked-- who had part time jobs and went to school-- to put their little bits of money together, so that they could have a down payment for a house. And when that house was purchased, everybody's name went on the title. My husband tried to advise them that this may not be the wisest thing to do. But, but they were so proud. They wanted to do it, and they did it. And they're doing fine. And, of course now, they're all married, and they all have children. The children have children. And so the family has grown, grown. Shandor, just a week ago, had a light stroke. But I think he'll be all right. I just talked to him. He was able to speak a little bit. And this, this naturally, was of some concern to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your children? Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sons, David and Jonathan. And I'm very proud of them. They're wonderful men. David is an MD. He practices internal medicine and also surgery in Modesto, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: David was born in 1953, and my younger son is Jonathan, who was born in 1955. Jonathan has five children, and he lives in Berkeley. He is a speech pathologist, a professional musician, and he also teaches summer school at Hayward State University-- English as a second language, culture, and history.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a Jewish education did you give your boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time when David was in elementary school, there was no other-- no alternative. There were no alternative schools in San Francisco. But I think he received a good education. He went to Hebrew school, and graduated also from Hebrew high school in addition to public school. He went to a college preparatory high school, for which he qualified because of his good grades. And by the time Jonathan was elementary school age, my husband Karl and a group of leaders in San Francisco, including some rabbis, had formed a group and founded the Brandeis Day School. Our younger son, Jonathan, was its first grad-- in its first graduation class. He received a very good-- he speaks Hebrew, of course, very well, and reads and writes it and in addition to a good secular education. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you affiliated with any synagogue at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We always have been. Karl and I are cons-- belong to the conservative movement of Judaism. That doesn't mean we are conservatives. It means that Judaism has three branches-- reform, conservative, and orthodox. And it has also the ultra orthodox, or Hasidim. So we are sort of right in the middle of this. And we've been members, and Karl has been president, for six years in our synagogue, and San Francisco in his earlier years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other organizational affiliations? Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Karl was also president of the Zionist Organization of America, Northern California region and for a number of years at the t-- and in its heyday. We-- I was always a member of Hadassah. When I first came, I joined Junior Hadassah. I actually want to follow my mother's footsteps, who was a member of WIZO in Europe. And Hadassah, at that time-- well, there was no Hadassah in Europe, but it was equivalent. WIZO and Hadassah was the same thing. When I came here, there was no WIZO. There was Hadassah, and so that was fine with me-- which is the Women's Zionist Movement Organization. And I think it's a very good organization. It helps-- it does a lot of good work in Israel, particularly with hospitals-- building hospitals, and maintaining staff and equipment, and helping a lot of people benefit from it in the entire Middle East, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you doing anything with regards to your experiences in the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I started speaking in schools in 1977, formally. And two years later, the Holocaust Center of Northern California was born. And I was asked to become a board member. So I am a member of its board of directors ever since. It is, especially lately, a growing organization. And it's-- it is a worthwhile organization. We have about 11,000 books, and some of them are Yizkor books. Yizkor books are books that were written about isolated communities that were wiped out during the Shoah, or Holocaust. And individuals, who-- a few people who survived got together and wrote about the community in which Jews lived before. Those are called Yizkor books.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm curious. You go to schools and explain the Shoah and your experiences in the Shoah. How did you explain your experiences to your own children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, at one time, wasn't so easy. When David, my first son-- first child-- began asking me, Mommy, what's that number on your arm? I had to give him an answer. I was still in college. And I remember I was absolutely dumbfounded. I could not understand how I could answer this child so that he could understand it. He was less than three years old-- perhaps even just 2 and 1/2-- around this period. Finally, my professor said, just give him answers that he can understand. And in short, don't go beyond that. Well, I understood that. The problem was that in libraries, there were no books in the '50s about the Holocaust. And survivors, including myself, had to simply use our instincts as to how to tell, or what to tell, about the Holocaust. It was a very difficult period.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very anxious, particularly knowing how hurtful the subject can be, that I do not pass this on to my child. I found a way. And, for example, I said, well, at one time, honey, I was incarcerated. And, and, but why? Well, simply because I was born Jewish. I didn't do anything wrong. Simply because I was born to Jewish parents and grandparents, they put me in jail. It was worse than jail because they, they went far beyond that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually I told him more and more. But the more I told him, the more he wanted to know. And one question led to another. And sometimes I had to tell him that I must tell him, answer him, some other time. And, of course, I-- it's still a difficult subject for me to tell children. That's why I don't-- I hardly ever accept a request to speak in elementary schools. I just have a hard time with it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As adults-- I remember when David first showed up as an adult at an event. I told him where I'm going to be, and I had a series of engagements on my calendar. And there he was, and he began to cry. And, of course, it-- just the very thought pains me. It was very difficult for my son to learn that his mother went through all that. And little did he know that these were not-- what the details were about these. And these, he was to find out much later from me. And my younger son did the same. Actually, my older son was much quieter about this. He was more introverted, whereas my younger son eventually began to talk about my experiences in his classes. I was so proud that he could do that. I wish that David could have open-- opened up, but this came later to David. And now, sometimes I get calls from his clients. I should come to the church to which they belong. And, and so he is with it. He is very proud of me, that I'm doing what I'm doing. And both of my sons recognize the importance of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering if there's anything you'd like to add that might be of importance to you? Well, I-- we had a little break a little while ago, and I thought, oh how, could I have left this out? A little-- a very painful story-- perhaps that's why-- that happened in Auschwitz. But it's something that, that the world must know about. While I was in Auschwitz one night, I woke up to screams. And we actually reached the point that screams didn't wake us up anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, I woke up, and I looked around. And there, right there by my bunk bed-- between the rows of bunk beds-- there were two women holding down another woman. And-- actually, not at first. But they were there. And there was-- there were about four inmates. And the lady was screaming because she was pregnant when she arrived. She was a newlywed, and she didn't know that she was pregnant. And so she didn't show it, show her pregnancy. But after about three months, she began to show. And so, apparently there was an underground in this area as well because they wanted to save her life, and take her baby away. And I learned that the l-- one of the ladies there was Dr. Gisi. Everybody called her Dr. Gisi, whom I was to meet later. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then finally, the wom-- they told her that, you should give up this baby. And someday, someday when we get out of this hellhole, that you and your husband will meet, and you can have another child. But if you don't consent to this, both you and your baby will be murdered, as you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so finally, she consented. And she cried very hard. And I actually-- I woke up to that, before the process started. And that, of course, became of interest to me. How could anybody go back to sleep after that? I, I watched the procedure as they took her baby away. She, with her bare hands, reached in and, and aborted the pregnancy so that the woman could live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many years later, just a few years ago-- maybe about 10, 15 years ago-- I was looking into some paper. And there was an advertisement in it that I-- about a book which said, And I decided to send for that book because it was the Shaare Zedek hospital in Jerusalem that put the ad in into the bulletin, and-- the Jewish bulletin in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the book arrived, and I was wondering if could it possibly be this doctor? But there were many doctors in Auschwitz. And sure enough, I read this book with so much eagerness, as you can well imagine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Dr. Gisi from Budapest, and I've learned so much about her that I didn't know before who sent me back to my mother in Auschwitz, to save my life. And she wrote in this book that she, she is now a pediatrician, and she is in Israel. And that she has given birth to hundreds-- I don't remember how many hundreds-- of children to make up for all of those that she has taken away in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gisella Perl. P-E-R-L. There's no A, Perl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Israel does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died a couple of years ago, I read in the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of her book? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was simply incredible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There is one item that I forgot. I was going to get you the name of, of the general manager of engineering at the Continental Gummi Werke in Hanover. It was a Dr. Jurgen, J-U-R-G-E-N, Robisch, R-O-B-I-S-C-H. He was a very nice gentleman, and he told us that he was only three years old at the time. And he knows nothing about this-- about this period, and that, as I have mentioned earlier, he suggested that we go to another person, Dr. von Herz-- that means from the heart in English. And so, that correspondence has dried up. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess this just about sums up my history. I was just wondering, you've done many speaking engagements to schools, and you've made a documentary called And I would like to know why you chose to do a tape for the Shoah Visual History of Steven Spielberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is very important to me.
